questions.stem: A child has a harsh systolic murmur at the left lower sternal border and recurrent chest infections. Which congenital lesion is most likely?
questions.explanation: VSD commonly causes a harsh pansystolic murmur at the left lower sternal border. Large defects can cause pulmonary overcirculation and recurrent respiratory infections.
wrong: Tetralogy of Fallot is a cyanotic lesion with right ventricular outflow obstruction and cyanotic spells.
PDA classically causes a continuous machinery murmur.
Coarctation causes upper limb hypertension and radio-femoral delay.
questions.takeaway: Harsh LLSB systolic murmur + pulmonary overcirculation = VSD.

questions.stem: A newborn with cyanosis improves when placed in a knee-chest position. Which condition is classically associated with this?
questions.explanation: The knee-chest position increases systemic vascular resistance, reducing right-to-left shunting in Tetralogy of Fallot and improving cyanosis during hypercyanotic spells.
wrong: ASD usually causes a fixed split S2 and is often not cyanotic early.
Small VSD is usually acyanotic and may close spontaneously.
PFO alone is usually clinically silent.
Mitral valve prolapse is not a cyanotic congenital heart disease.
questions.takeaway: Cyanotic spell relieved by knee-chest = Tetralogy of Fallot.

questions.stem: Which congenital heart disease classically produces a continuous machinery murmur?
questions.explanation: PDA produces continuous flow from the aorta to the pulmonary artery throughout systole and diastole, causing a continuous machinery murmur.
wrong: ASD produces fixed splitting of S2 with a systolic flow murmur.
Pulmonary stenosis causes an ejection systolic murmur.
Aortic stenosis causes an ejection systolic murmur radiating to the neck.
